summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that uploading a task attachment with an .html filename causes the server to respond with Content-Type: text/html when the attachment is downloaded, enabling stored cross-site scripting in the browser.
expected_security_impact: Uploading an attachment named xss-probe.html containing a JavaScript payload, then downloading it, should result in a response with Content-Type: text/html instead of application/octet-stream. This allows the browser to render and execute the embedded JavaScript.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh built the Vikunja binary, started the server, uploaded an attachment with filename 'xss-probe.html' containing '<script>document.write("EVOHUNT-XSS-PROOF")</script>', then downloaded the attachment. The response Content-Type header was text/html; charset=utf-8, confirming the MIME type confusion vulnerability.
verification_method: The verify script uploads an HTML file as a task attachment, then downloads it using curl with the -I flag to inspect response headers. It checks that the Content-Type header starts with 'text/html', which distinguishes the vulnerability from the expected safe behavior (application/octet-stream or the file's actual MIME type).
evidence_produced_by_run: The attachment upload succeeded and the file was stored with the attacker-chosen filename 'xss-probe.html'.
The attachment download response included Content-Type: text/html; charset=utf-8, proving the MIME type is derived from the user-controlled filename extension.
The response body contained the unmodified '<script>document.write("EVOHUNT-XSS-PROOF")</script>' payload, which a browser would execute as JavaScript.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned ref, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
